frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
A: Arre bhai, main itna hi maanta hoon ki shaadi ke baad thoda sindoor ya mangalsutra pehne to aurat suhagan lagti hai, ghar-grihasthi wali feeling aati hai.
B: Haan, but aajkal many girls say it's personal choice.
A: Choice apni jagah hai, par hamari parampara bhi koi cheez hoti hai. Ye sab pati ke mangal aur shaadi ki pehchan ke symbol hain, bina uske thoda ajeeb hi lagta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is covert misogyny because it frames visible marital markers as natural, auspicious, and part of tradition rather than as control. It encodes completeness framing, tradition framing, and judgment of women who do not wear sindoor or mangalsutra.
original_frame: Other Man's Ownership Over Woman's Own Consent.
persona_text: You are Deepak. Age 39. Married. An upper caste Hindu man in Bhopal. You are sitting with four male colleagues in the office canteen during lunch. One colleague is telling the group that his 25 year old daughter told him she is in a live-in relationship with her boyfriend. You are one of four men reacting to this news. You are giving your opinion on live-in relationships and what a father's responsibility is when his daughter makes choices like this and what it says about how she was raised.
